narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 49% chance that you might fail the bar exam on your first try, which is the threshold we use to make admission decisions. This prediction is based on a careful look at key aspects of your academic and background profile, including your low ranking in the bottom decile for grades in both your first and third years of school—far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack position—along with your LSAT score of 29, which is notably lower than the average of around 37. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.40 stands out as stronger than the typical 3.21, and your middle-class family income aligns closely with what's common, while being a full-time female Asian student matches patterns seen in most applicants. When the model weighed these factors to forecast your bar exam success, your LSAT score had the strongest influence, pushing the prediction toward a higher risk of failure because it was so much lower than what usually leads to passing. Right behind that, your bottom-decile ranking in first-year grades played a big role in the same direction, signaling potential challenges in early academic habits. Your third-year decile ranking, also at the bottom, was the next most influential, reinforcing concerns about consistent performance over time. Family income followed as a notable factor nudging toward failure risk, even at a middle level, perhaps due to how it correlates with resources in the dataset. Your racial background as Asian had a moderate pull in that direction compared to the mostly White typical students, while your above-average undergrad GPA still contributed somewhat to the failure prediction in this context—maybe because it wasn't enough to offset the other lows. Being full-time and female had the smallest influences but still leaned slightly toward the risk side relative to averages. Overall, these stacked up to tip the scales against you, unlike many applicants whose stronger test scores and school rankings pull them toward predicted success. To improve your chances next time, focus on what you can control: retake the LSAT to aim for a score closer to or above 37, as it's the top factor and highly changeable with targeted prep. Building better study strategies could help demonstrate improved performance in future academics, potentially lifting those decile rankings in grad-level work. Keep leveraging your solid undergrad GPA as a foundation, and consider part-time options if they fit better, though full-time is common—reapplying with these boosts could flip the prediction in your favor. Hang in there; many students turn things around with focused effort.
